roof_system: Metal roof field with a separate lighter low-slope roof area; primary roof field appears to be metal panel roofing

possible_roof_systems.evidence: Long parallel standing-seam/panel-like bands with alternating light/dark reflections across the main roof field are consistent with a metal roof surface.

The lighter central roof area appears flat/low-slope and uniform from aerial view, but the banded appearance could also reflect a membrane roof with panelized shadowing; imagery is not sufficient to confirm.

roof_age_estimate: Newer roof, likely within a few years of 2018 given the 2017 construction date and generally serviceable appearance

notes: Several rooftop penetrations/equipment items are visible on the lighter roof area, increasing flashing and seal maintenance needs.
No obvious widespread staining, algae-like discoloration, or ponding is visible from this aerial image.
Roof field appears generally uniform and maintained.
Aerial imagery is dated 03/11/2018 - 03/17/2018; current conditions may differ.

observations: The dominant roof surface shows repeated linear bands and reflective striping consistent with a metal panel roof field.
A separate lighter low-slope roof area is visible with a few penetrations/equipment items near the upper section.
No clear widespread membrane discoloration, dark staining, or debris accumulation is visible.
No obvious ponding areas are apparent from the overhead imagery.
Perimeter and adjacent site areas do not show overhanging trees contacting the roof.
summary: Aerial imagery from 03/11/2018 - 03/17/2018 shows a generally serviceable industrial roof with a dominant metal roof field and a separate lighter low-slope roof area. Visible risk is mainly from the limited number of rooftop penetrations/equipment on the low-slope section, which increases flashing and seal vulnerability. No clear widespread staining, debris accumulation, or obvious ponding is visible in the image, though older imagery may understate current wear.
recommendation: The roof appears generally sound from aerial view, but the visible penetrations and low-slope areas warrant an onsite commercial roof inspection to confirm flashing, seams, and drainage performance. If the lighter low-slope section is a qualifying membrane roof and remains restorable, a silicone roof restoration could be evaluated before a more costly overlay or replacement, as a properly designed system may reduce tear-off waste, cost, and disruption while creating a seamless reflective surface and improving UV/ponding resistance; confirm system compatibility, moisture conditions, repairs, and warranty eligibility with a qualified commercial roof-coating contractor. If onsite inspection finds hidden moisture, substrate problems, or incompatible materials, then overlay or replacement evaluation would be more appropriate.
